overall_topic: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder: What You Need to Know
questions: In the context of United States federal education policy regarding students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), identify the primary statute that mandates and governs special education and related services. Since this statute does not list ADHD as a distinct, standalone disability category, determine the specific classification under which students with this condition most commonly qualify for services. Additionally, name the specific legally binding document that must be developed to outline the educational goals and services for a student qualifying under this law.

Identify the primary United States federal statute that governs how states and public agencies provide early intervention, special education, and related services to eligible children with disabilities. Specifically, determine the classification category under which a student with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) most frequently qualifies for services under this law, provided they do not have another primary disability. Furthermore, distinguish between the specific age groups covered by Part B and Part C of this legislation.

Identify the specific office within the U.S. Department of Education tasked with enforcing federal civil rights laws, which is led by an Assistant Secretary appointed with the advice and consent of the Senate. According to its enforcement mandate, list the specific types of non-school institutions (such as cultural or corrective facilities) that fall under its jurisdiction. Additionally, regarding the federal statutes protecting disability rights: (1) Name the two specific categories of grants defined under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act, and (2) Explain the specific focus of the recent updates to Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act regarding state and local government obligations.

Identify the federal office within the Department of Education described as enforcing civil rights laws and managing a staff across regional offices, and the specific federal act that mandates 'Section 618 Data Products' for disability education. Based on the provided text, list the locations of the regional enforcement offices specifically named for that civil rights office (including the one co-located with headquarters). Additionally, enumerate the seven specific 'Static Files' data categories available for 'Part B' under the identified Act's data products as listed in the Open Data Platform migration details.

Identify the federal office tasked with enforcing civil rights laws in educational institutions receiving federal funds. According to its specific investigative authority, what are the two distinct conditions under which 'Retaliation' is actionable, and what specific statutory and age-based criteria define the 'Youth Groups' protected from discrimination regarding meeting access? Additionally, list the specific 'Centers', 'Clearinghouses', and 'Laboratories' managed by this office's parent department to conduct research, specifically noting which center focuses on infants and youth with disabilities.

rounds.question: In the context of United States federal education policy regarding students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), identify the primary statute that mandates and governs special education and related services. Since this statute does not list ADHD as a distinct, standalone disability category, determine the specific classification under which students with this condition most commonly qualify for services. Additionally, name the specific legally binding document that must be developed to outline the educational goals and services for a student qualifying under this law.
checklist_width: Identifies the specific eligibility classification as 'Other Health Impairment' (OHI)
Identifies the required document as an 'Individualized Education Program' (IEP)
checklist_depth: Identifies the primary statute as the 'Individuals with Disabilities Education Act' (IDEA)
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by describing the function of the law (governing special education for ADHD) without naming it. It requires Wide Aggregation by demanding specific implementation details (the precise eligibility category and the specific document name) that usually require reading specific subsections or regulations of the law regarding ADHD.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) [Wide Logic - Target 1]:
rounds.answer_a:
At the federal level, the principal statute that mandates and governs special education and related services for eligible students, including those with ADHD, is the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), codified at 20 U.S.C. §1400 et seq. [1][2]. IDEA and its regulations list specific disability categories, but ADHD is not a standalone category; instead, students whose ADHD adversely affects their educational performance most commonly qualify under the category “Other Health Impairment” (OHI), which explicitly includes attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder in its definition at 34 C.F.R. §300.8(c)(9) [2][3].  

Once a student is found eligible under IDEA (often through the OHI category for ADHD), the school district is legally required to develop, review, and revise as needed an Individualized Education Program (IEP). The IEP is a binding written document that specifies the student’s present levels of academic achievement and functional performance, measurable annual goals, the special education and related services, accommodations, and the extent of participation in the general education setting that the public agency must provide [1][4].  

## References list  

[1] U.S. Department of Education. “A Guide to the Individualized Education Program.” https://www2.ed.gov/parents/needs/speced/iepguide/index.html  

[2] U.S. Department of Education. “About IDEA.” https://sites.ed.gov/idea/about-idea/  

[3] 34 C.F.R. §300.8(c)(9), “Other health impairment.” eCFR. https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-34/part-300/section-300.8  

[4] U.S. Department of Education, OCR & OSERS. “Dear Colleague Letter and Resource Guide on ADHD.” 2016. https://www2.ed.gov/about/offices/list/ocr/letters/colleague-201607-504-adhd.pdf
rounds.answer_b:
The controlling federal law is the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), 20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq., which requires public schools to provide eligible students with free, appropriate special-education and related services [1]. Because IDEA does not name Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder as a stand-alone disability, students with ADHD most commonly qualify under the category “Other Health Impairment” (OHI), defined as having limited strength, vitality, or alertness—including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli—due to chronic or acute health problems such as ADHD that adversely affect educational performance [2]. Once a student is found eligible under the OHI category, the school district, parents, and relevant professionals must collaboratively create an Individualized Education Program (IEP). The IEP is a legally enforceable document that specifies the student’s present levels of performance, measurable annual goals, specialized instruction, accommodations, and any related or supplementary services to be provided, and it must be reviewed and revised at least annually [3].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education. “A Guide to the Individualized Education Program,” IDEA statute overview.  
[2] 34 C.F.R. § 300.8(c)(9) – “Other health impairment” definition includes ADHD.  
[3] U.S. Department of Education. “Individualized Education Program (IEP) Process” factsheet.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents successfully identified all three ground truth elements: the statute (IDEA), the classification (Other Health Impairment/OHI), and the document (Individualized Education Program/IEP). Agent A is rated better primarily due to formatting and presentation. Agent A separates the legal eligibility context from the documentation requirements into two distinct paragraphs, making it easier to scan and digest. Agent B presents the answer as a single block of text. Furthermore, Agent A provides full, clickable URLs in its reference list, offering better utility than Agent B's descriptive citations.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

It enforces Wide Aggregation by requiring the retrieval of specific, distinct details: the exact legal category for ADHD (Other Health Impairment) and the demographic distinctions between the law's two main sections (Part B and Part C), which often requires synthesizing information from different sections of the law or detailed summaries.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: About IDEA - Individuals with Disabilities Education Act
rounds.answer_a:
The primary United States federal statute governing how states and public agencies provide early intervention, special education, and related services to eligible children with disabilities is the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), codified at 20 U.S.C. §1400 et seq. [1]. IDEA establishes both the right to a free appropriate public education (FAPE) and the procedures states must use to identify, evaluate, and serve children with disabilities.

Under IDEA’s disability classification system, Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is not listed as a separate category. Instead, when ADHD is the primary condition and it adversely affects a student’s educational performance, the student most frequently qualifies under the category of “Other Health Impairment” (OHI) [2]. Federal regulations define OHI to include chronic or acute health conditions such as attention deficit disorder or attention deficit hyperactivity disorder that result in limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli [2].

IDEA is divided into several parts. Part B covers special education and related services for “children with disabilities” generally between ages 3 and 21, inclusive, though the exact upper age can vary slightly by state depending on how they define the obligation to provide FAPE [1][3]. In contrast, Part C establishes a statewide early intervention system for “infants and toddlers with disabilities,” defined in federal law as children from birth up to, but not including, age 3 and their families [1][3].

Thus, a young child with ADHD-like symptoms who is under age 3 would potentially receive supports through an individualized family service plan (IFSP) under Part C, while a preschool or school-age student with ADHD whose educational performance is adversely affected would typically receive special education and related services under Part B, most often via an Individualized Education Program (IEP) based on eligibility in the OHI category.

## References list  

[1] U.S. Department of Education. “About IDEA.” https://sites.ed.gov/idea/about-idea/  

[2] 34 C.F.R. §300.8(c)(9) “Other health impairment.” https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-34/part-300/section-300.8  

[3] U.S. Department of Education. “Building the Legacy: IDEA 2004 – Early Intervening, Early Intervention, and Part C.” and related Part B guidance (age ranges). https://sites.ed.gov/idea/The primary United States federal statute governing how states and public agencies provide early intervention, special education, and related services to eligible children with disabilities is the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), codified at 20 U.S.C. §1400 et seq. [1]. IDEA establishes both the right to a free appropriate public education (FAPE) and the procedures states must use to identify, evaluate, and serve children with disabilities.

Under IDEA’s disability classification system, Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is not listed as a separate category. Instead, when ADHD is the primary condition and it adversely affects a student’s educational performance, the student most frequently qualifies under the category of “Other Health Impairment” (OHI) [2]. Federal regulations define OHI to include chronic or acute health conditions such as attention deficit disorder or attention deficit hyperactivity disorder that result in limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli [2].

IDEA is divided into several parts. Part B covers special education and related services for “children with disabilities” generally between ages 3 and 21, inclusive, though the exact upper age can vary slightly by state depending on how they define the obligation to provide FAPE [1][3]. In contrast, Part C establishes a statewide early intervention system for “infants and toddlers with disabilities,” defined in federal law as children from birth up to, but not including, age 3 and their families [1][3].

Thus, a young child with ADHD-like symptoms who is under age 3 would potentially receive supports through an individualized family service plan (IFSP) under Part C, while a preschool or school-age student with ADHD whose educational performance is adversely affected would typically receive special education and related services under Part B, most often via an Individualized Education Program (IEP) based on eligibility in the OHI category.

## References list  

[1] U.S. Department of Education. “About IDEA.” https://sites.ed.gov/idea/about-idea/  

[2] 34 C.F.R. §300.8(c)(9) “Other health impairment.” https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-34/part-300/section-300.8  

[3] U.S. Department of Education. “Building the Legacy: IDEA 2004 – Early Intervening, Early Intervention, and Part C.” and related Part B guidance (age ranges). https://sites.ed.gov/idea/
rounds.answer_b:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), 20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq., is the principal federal law directing how states and public agencies deliver early-intervention, special-education, and related services to eligible children with disabilities. When Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder is the child’s primary challenge, eligibility most commonly arises under the IDEA disability category “Other Health Impairment” (OHI). Federal regulations define OHI as having “limited strength, vitality, or alertness … due to chronic or acute health problems such as … attention-deficit disorder or attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder” that adversely affect educational performance [1].

IDEA distinguishes two age-based service frameworks:

• Part C (Early Intervention): Applies to “infants and toddlers with disabilities,” birth through age 2. States electing Part C funds must identify, evaluate, and serve eligible children via an Individualized Family Service Plan (IFSP) that sets developmental goals and services, typically provided in natural environments such as the home or community settings [2].

• Part B (Special Education): Covers “children with disabilities” ages 3 through 21 (or until high-school graduation). Eligible students—including those qualifying under OHI for ADHD—receive a Free Appropriate Public Education documented in an Individualized Education Program (IEP). The IEP outlines present performance levels, measurable annual goals, specialized instruction, accommodations, and any related services needed to enable the student to progress in the general curriculum [3].

In summary, IDEA governs all federal special-education and early-intervention services; students whose chief disability is ADHD typically qualify under the OHI category; Part C provides services from birth through age 2, while Part B provides services from age 3 up to 21.  

## References  
[1] 34 C.F.R. § 300.8(c)(9) (definition of “Other health impairment”).  
[2] U.S. Department of Education, “Early Intervention Program for Infants and Toddlers with Disabilities (Part C),” ed.gov, 2023.  
[3] U.S. Department of Education, “A Guide to the Individualized Education Program,” ed.gov, 2020.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the statute (IDEA), the classification for ADHD (OHI), and the age ranges for Part B and Part C. However, Agent B provided a significantly better user experience through superior formatting. Agent B used bullet points to clearly distinguish between Part B and Part C, which directly addressed the prompt's request to 'distinguish between' the groups. Agent A used dense paragraphs, making the comparison harder to scan. Additionally, Agent A appeared to have a generation glitch where the text was repeated/concatenated at the end of the response.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: OCR vigilantly enforces federal civil rights laws in schools and other recipients of Department of Education funding throughout the nation, managing and sustaining an expert s
rounds.answer_a:
The office is the U.S. Department of Education’s Office for Civil Rights (OCR), headed by the Assistant Secretary for Civil Rights, a presidential appointee confirmed by the Senate. OCR enforces federal civil rights laws prohibiting discrimination based on race, color, national origin, sex (including pregnancy, sexual orientation, and gender identity), disability, and age in programs receiving federal funds from the Department of Education or, for disability, in certain public entities under Title II of the ADA as delegated by DOJ [1][2].

OCR’s jurisdiction extends beyond traditional schools to a range of non‑school institutions that receive ED funds. These include libraries, museums, cultural centers, vocational rehabilitation agencies, state agencies overseeing education services, correctional institutions and prisons providing educational programs, juvenile justice facilities, and other public or private entities that operate education or training programs with ED financial assistance [1][2].

Under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), federal financial support is organized into two main grant categories:  
1. **Formula grants** to states (and, under Part B, to local educational agencies) to help cover the costs of providing special education and related services to children with disabilities (primarily Parts B and C) [3].  
2. **Discretionary grants** for research, personnel preparation, technical assistance, model demonstration projects, and parent training and information centers (primarily under Part D) [3].

Recent updates to regulations under Title II of the Americans with Disabilities Act, issued by the U.S. Department of Justice in 2024, focus specifically on state and local governments’ obligations to ensure the accessibility of their **websites and mobile applications**. The rule establishes enforceable technical standards—principally by requiring conformance with the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 Level AA—along with phased compliance deadlines and limited exceptions, so that people with disabilities can equally access online public services, benefits, and information provided by state and local governments [4][5].

## References list  

[1] U.S. Department of Education, Office for Civil Rights – “About OCR & Mission.”  
[2] U.S. Department of Education – “Know Your Rights: OCR’s Jurisdiction and Enforcement Responsibilities.”  
[3] U.S. Department of Education – “About IDEA: Types of Grants under IDEA (Formula and Discretionary).”  
[4] U.S. Department of Justice – “Nondiscrimination on the Basis of Disability; Accessibility of Web Information and Services of State and Local Government Entities” (Title II final rule, 2024).  
[5] ADA.gov – Fact Sheet on the Title II Web and Mobile App Accessibility Rule.
rounds.answer_b:
The Office for Civil Rights (OCR) within the U.S. Department of Education is the unit charged with enforcing the Department’s civil-rights statutes. It is headed by an Assistant Secretary for Civil Rights who is nominated by the President and confirmed by the Senate [1].

OCR’s jurisdiction covers any program or activity receiving ED financial assistance. Beyond elementary, secondary, and post-secondary schools, this reach explicitly includes the following non-school institutions:
• Public libraries and library systems  
• Museums and cultural centers  
• State or local vocational-rehabilitation agencies and training centers  
• Proprietary schools and apprenticeship programs that receive ED funds  
• Correctional and juvenile-justice facilities (prisons, jails, detention centers) that provide educational services funded by ED grants [2].

Federal disability-rights statutes create additional obligations:

1. Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA).  IDEA authorizes two formula-grant categories:  
   a. “Grants to States” (Section 611) – funds preschool-through-grade-12 services for children with disabilities ages 3-21.  
   b. “Preschool Grants” (Section 619) – supplemental funds targeted exclusively to children with disabilities ages 3-5 [3].

2. Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Title II.  In April 2024 the U.S. Department of Justice issued a final rule modernizing Title II’s regulations for state and local governments.  The update focuses on digital accessibility: websites, web-based services, and mobile applications offered by public entities must conform to the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1, Level AA.  Compliance deadlines are phased by population size (two to three years for larger entities; up to four years for small jurisdictions) with limited exemptions (e.g., archived content, third-party web material not under the entity’s control).  The rule clarifies that inaccessible digital content can constitute discrimination, thereby obligating governments to proactively ensure equal access rather than relying solely on after-the-fact accommodations [4].

Taken together, OCR’s broad institutional reach, IDEA’s grant structure, and DOJ’s strengthened digital-accessibility standards delineate the contemporary federal framework safeguarding civil-rights and disability access in U.S. education and public services.

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Education, “Office for Civil Rights—About OCR,” ed.gov, 2023.  
[2] U.S. Department of Education, OCR, “Programs and Activities Subject to Civil Rights Laws,” ed.gov, 2023.  
[3] U.S. Department of Education, “IDEA Formula Grant Programs: Grants to States (Sec. 611) and Preschool Grants (Sec. 619),” ed.gov, 2024.  
[4] U.S. Department of Justice, “DOJ Issues Final Rule to Strengthen Web and Mobile App Accessibility Under Title II of the ADA,” press release, 24 Apr 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A correctly identifies the two broad categories of grants under IDEA (Formula and Discretionary). Agent B incorrectly interprets this part of the prompt by listing two specific sub-programs within the Formula category (Section 611 and 619), completely missing the Discretionary grants category. Both agents handled the OCR jurisdiction and ADA Title II updates well, but Agent A's accuracy on the IDEA classification makes it the winner.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: OCR vigilantly enforces federal civil rights laws in schools and other recipients of Department of Education funding throughout the nation, managing and sustaining an expert s
rounds.answer_a:
The federal office is the **Office for Civil Rights (OCR)** within the U.S. Department of Education. OCR is led by the Assistant Secretary for Civil Rights (a presidential appointee confirmed by the Senate) and enforces federal civil rights laws in education, managing a nationwide staff distributed across multiple regional enforcement offices [1].

In the description of OCR’s structure, the following specific regional enforcement office locations are named, including the office co‑located with headquarters:  
- **Washington, D.C.** (headquarters and enforcement office)  
- **Boston, Massachusetts**  
- **New York, New York**  
- **Philadelphia, Pennsylvania**  
- **Atlanta, Georgia**  
- **Chicago, Illinois**  
- **Dallas, Texas**  
- **Kansas City, Missouri**  
- **Denver, Colorado**  
- **San Francisco, California**  
- **Seattle, Washington**  
- **Cleveland, Ohio** [1].

The federal act that mandates the “**Section 618 Data Products**” is the **Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)**. Section 618 of IDEA requires states to submit specific data on children with disabilities served under Parts B and C, which the Office of Special Education Programs (OSEP) publishes as IDEA Section 618 Data Products [2][3].

In the Open Data Platform migration materials for IDEA Section 618, the **seven “Static Files” data categories for Part B** are listed as follows [3][4]:

1. **Child Count and Educational Environments** (Part B)  
2. **Exiting** (Part B)  
3. **Discipline** (Part B)  
4. **Personnel** (Part B)  
5. **Assessment** (Part B)  
6. **Dispute Resolution** (Part B)  
7. **Maintenance of Effort (MOE) Reduction and Coordinated Early Intervening Services (CEIS)** (Part B)

These static files are the historical, fixed-format datasets that were migrated or are being migrated into the newer IDEA Open Data Platform.

## References list  

[1] U.S. Department of Education, Office for Civil Rights. “About OCR – Offices and Staff / OCR Locations.”  
[2] 20 U.S.C. §1418 (IDEA Section 618 – Program information).  
[3] U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs. “IDEA Section 618 Data Products: Static Files and Open Data Platform Migration.”  
[4] OSEP IDEA Data Center / IDEA Open Data Platform – “Part B Static Files: Data Categories and Migration Overview.”
rounds.answer_b:
The Office for Civil Rights (OCR) in the U.S. Department of Education, led by an Assistant Secretary confirmed by the Senate, enforces federal civil-rights laws and supervises a nationwide staff through 12 regional enforcement offices. The statute that establishes the “Section 618 Data Products” is the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), which compels the Department to collect, analyze, and publish annual data on children with disabilities served under Parts B and C.

Regional OCR enforcement-office locations (including the unit co-located with headquarters) are:

• Washington, D.C. (headquarters/enforcement office)  
• Boston, Massachusetts  
• New York, New York  
• Philadelphia, Pennsylvania  
• Cleveland, Ohio  
• Chicago, Illinois  
• Atlanta, Georgia  
• Dallas, Texas  
• Kansas City, Missouri  
• Denver, Colorado  
• San Francisco, California  
• Seattle, Washington  

IDEA’s Open Data Platform migration lists seven “Part B – Static Files” data categories that states must submit and the Department must release:

1. Child Count and Educational Environments – point-in-time counts of students with disabilities and their placement settings.  
2. Personnel – numbers of special-education teachers, paraprofessionals, and related-service providers employed or contracted by districts.  
3. Exiting – outcomes for students leaving special education (graduates, dropouts, age-outs, transfers, deaths).  
4. Discipline – suspensions, expulsions, removals to interim alternative settings, and incidents of restraint or seclusion.  
5. Assessment – participation and proficiency of students with disabilities on statewide general and alternate assessments.  
6. Dispute Resolution – counts of state complaints, due-process complaints, mediations, and resolution sessions, with their results.  
7. Maintenance of Effort (MOE) Reduction and Coordinated Early Intervening Services (CEIS) – fiscal reports showing districts that reduced local spending under MOE rules and the students served with CEIS funds.

Together, OCR’s geographically dispersed enforcement network and IDEA’s Section 618 data mandate embody the federal government’s dual strategy of proactive civil-rights oversight and public transparency regarding the education of children with disabilities.  

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Education, Office for Civil Rights. “Contact OCR” (regional office directory), ed.gov, 2024.  
[2] Individuals with Disabilities Education Act, 20 U.S.C. § 1418 (Section 618).  
[3] U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs. “IDEA Section 618 Data Products: Open Data Platform Migration,” ed.gov, 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the entities (OCR and IDEA), listed the 12 regional offices, and enumerated the 7 Part B data categories. Agent A is rated better primarily due to superior formatting and citation practices. Agent A used bold text for the list items (offices and data categories), making the response significantly more scannable. Additionally, Agent A included inline citations (e.g., [1]) to verify specific claims, whereas Agent B only provided a reference list at the end without linking sources to the text.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: Identify the federal office tasked with enforcing civil rights laws in educational institutions receiving federal funds. According to its specific investigative authority, what are the two distinct conditions under which 'Retaliation' is actionable, and what specific statutory and age-based criteria define the 'Youth Groups' protected from discrimination regarding meeting access? Additionally, list the specific 'Centers', 'Clearinghouses', and 'Laboratories' managed by this office's parent department to conduct research, specifically noting which center focuses on infants and youth with disabilities.
checklist_width: Retaliation Condition 1: Action taken for the purpose of interfering with any right or privilege protected by laws enforced by the office.
Retaliation Condition 2: Action taken because someone made a complaint, testified, assisted, or participated in a matter.
Youth Group Criterion 1: Officially affiliated with a group/organization listed in Title 36 of the U.S. Code (Patriotic Society).
Youth Group Criterion 2: Intended to serve young people under the age of 21.
Youth Group Criterion 3: Requests to conduct a meeting at a public school.
Research Center 1: National Center for Education Evaluations.
Research Center 2: National Center for Special Education Research (NCSER) (Specifically for infants/youth with disabilities).
Clearinghouse: What Works Clearinghouse (WWC).
Laboratory: Regional Educational Laboratory Program (REL).
Library/Resource: Education Resources Information Center (ERIC).
checklist_depth: Target Entity: Office for Civil Rights (OCR).

Logic Proof: The entity is identified via its mission to enforce civil rights laws and its specific authority to investigate retaliation and youth group discrimination as described in the source text.
rounds.rationale: The query requires Deep reasoning to identify the Office for Civil Rights (OCR) based on its mission and enforcement mandate rather than its name. It then enforces Wide aggregation by requiring the user to extract granular legal definitions of 'Retaliation' and 'Youth Groups' from one section of the hidden text (Target 3) while simultaneously retrieving and categorizing a list of research entities (Centers, Clearinghouses, Labs) from a separate section (Target 1).

rounds.answer_a:
The federal office is the U.S. Department of Education’s **Office for Civil Rights (OCR)**, which enforces federal civil rights laws in education programs and activities receiving Department of Education funds.[1]

Under OCR’s investigative authority, **retaliation** is actionable when:  
1. A person suffers an adverse action (e.g., intimidation, threats, coercion, harassment, or other discrimination) **because they filed a complaint, assisted, or participated in any manner in an OCR complaint, investigation, or proceeding** (participation clause).[2]  
2. A person suffers an adverse action **because they opposed an act or practice made unlawful by the civil rights laws OCR enforces, or asserted rights protected by those laws** (opposition/assertion-of-rights clause).[2]

The **“Youth Groups”** protected from discrimination in meeting access are defined by the **Boy Scouts of America Equal Access Act**, which OCR enforces under Title IX regulations. A youth group is:  
- Any group or organization **intended to serve young people under the age of 21**, and  
- That is listed in **Title 36 of the U.S. Code as a “patriotic society”** (e.g., Boy Scouts of America, Girl Scouts of the USA, etc.).[3]  
Public elementary and secondary schools receiving federal funds that provide access to any outside youth or community group must not deny equal access or fair opportunity to such qualifying youth groups to conduct meetings at the school.[3]

OCR’s parent department, the **U.S. Department of Education**, conducts education research primarily through the **Institute of Education Sciences (IES)**, which manages the following **Centers**:  
- **National Center for Education Statistics (NCES)**  
- **National Center for Education Evaluation and Regional Assistance (NCEE)**  
- **National Center for Education Research (NCER)**  
- **National Center for Special Education Research (NCSER)** – this center specifically focuses on research regarding **infants, toddlers, children, and youth with disabilities**.[4]

IES also oversees key **Clearinghouses** and **Laboratories**, including:  
- **What Works Clearinghouse (WWC)**  
- **Education Resources Information Center (ERIC)**  
- **Regional Educational Laboratories (RELs)** – a network of ten regional labs that conduct applied research and provide technical assistance.[4][5]

## References list  

[1] U.S. Dept. of Education, Office for Civil Rights – “About OCR.”  
[2] U.S. Dept. of Education, OCR, Case Processing Manual – Retaliation provisions.  
[3] 20 U.S.C. §7905 (Boy Scouts of America Equal Access Act); ED/OCR guidance on Equal Access.  
[4] Institute of Education Sciences (IES) – “About IES” (centers and mission).  
[5] IES – “What Works Clearinghouse,” “ERIC,” and “Regional Educational Laboratories” descriptions.
rounds.answer_b:
The U.S. Department of Education’s Office for Civil Rights (OCR) enforces the federal civil-rights statutes that apply to any educational program or activity receiving Department funds.  

Actionable retaliation under OCR’s investigative authority arises in either of two circumstances:  
1. Adverse action against an individual because the person has made a complaint, testified, assisted, or otherwise participated in an OCR or internal grievance proceeding concerning discrimination.  
2. Intimidation, threats, coercion, or other discrimination directed at any individual for the purpose of interfering with that person’s exercise of rights secured by the laws OCR enforces.  Either condition violates 34 C.F.R. § 100.7(e) and parallel provisions in Title IX, Section 504, the ADA, and the Age Discrimination Act [1].

“Youth Groups” entitled to equal meeting access are defined by the Boy Scouts of America Equal Access to Public School Facilities Act, 20 U.S.C. § 7905.  A protected group must (a) be a “patriotic youth organization” chartered under 36 U.S.C. Subtitle II, Part B and (b) be “intended to serve young people under the age of 21 years.”  Public schools that permit outside organizations to meet on campus may not deny such a youth group equal access or a fair opportunity to meet, or discriminate against it in scheduling or use of facilities [2].

To advance evidence-based practice, the Department—through the Institute of Education Sciences—operates the following research entities:

National Research Centers  
• National Center for Education Research (NCER)  
• National Center for Education Statistics (NCES)  
• National Center for Education Evaluation and Regional Assistance (NCEE)  
• National Center for Special Education Research (NCSER) – the only center dedicated to studies on infants, toddlers, children, and youth with disabilities [3].

Clearinghouses  
• Education Resources Information Center (ERIC)  
• What Works Clearinghouse (WWC) [4].

Regional Educational Laboratories (RELs)  
• REL Appalachia • REL Central • REL Mid-Atlantic • REL Midwest • REL Northeast & Islands • REL Northwest • REL Pacific • REL Southeast • REL Southwest • REL West [5].

These centers, clearinghouses, and laboratories provide the research infrastructure that supports OCR’s broader mission of ensuring equal educational opportunity for all students.  

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Education, Office for Civil Rights, Case Processing Manual §108 (2023).  
[2] 20 U.S.C. § 7905; U.S. Department of Education, “Boy Scouts of America Equal Access Act” guidance (2022).  
[3] Institute of Education Sciences, “About the Centers,” ies.ed.gov (2024).  
[4] IES, “ERIC” and “What Works Clearinghouse” program pages, ies.ed.gov (2024).  
[5] IES, “Regional Educational Laboratory Program,” ies.ed.gov/rel (2024).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents provided highly accurate, comprehensive answers that met all constraints and checklist items. Agent A is preferred for superior formatting and user experience. 

1. **Formatting & Scannability**: Agent A used bolding effectively to highlight the specific criteria requested (e.g., "under the age of 21", "Title 36"), whereas Agent B buried these details in paragraph text. Agent A also used bullet points for the Youth Group criteria, making it easier to parse.
2. **Helpfulness**: For the Laboratories, Agent A listed the network (RELs) and described their function, which provides better context. Agent B listed every specific regional laboratory (REL Appalachia, REL Central, etc.), which, while technically accurate to the word "specific", resulted in a cluttered list that added less value than Agent A's contextual description.
